purpose: This policy describes how <<LAB_NAME>> identifies, documents, and resolves problems in pre-analytic, analytic, and post-analytic processes. Corrective action is required by 42 CFR 493.1282 (analytic systems) and is operationalized through QC failure response, PT investigation, and non-conformance event review.

cfr_text_blocks.label: Standard: Corrective actions
cfr_text_blocks.verbatim: The laboratory must have and follow corrective action policies and procedures to ensure that: (1) Test systems perform as required throughout the patient's test result reporting process. (2) Patient test results are accurate, reliable, and prompt, and confidentiality of patient data is preserved. (3) When errors in the reported patient test results are detected, the laboratory does the following: (i) Promptly notifies the authorized person ordering the test and, if applicable, the individual using the test results, of reporting errors. (ii) Issues corrected reports promptly to the authorized person ordering the test and, if applicable, the individual using the test results. (iii) Maintains duplicates of the original report, as well as the corrected report.

cfr_text_blocks.label: Standard: Corrective actions documentation
cfr_text_blocks.verbatim: The laboratory must document all corrective actions taken when test systems deviate from the laboratory's established performance specifications.

scope: All <<LAB_NAME>> testing personnel, the medical director or designee, the technical consultant or technical supervisor, and the quality coordinator (if assigned).
policy_statements: <<LAB_NAME>> documents every corrective action taken in response to a deviation from established performance specifications, a QC failure, a PT failure, a complaint, or any other quality finding.

Corrective action records capture: trigger (what was observed), root cause (why it happened), action taken (what was done), responsible person (who did it), date and time, verification (how the action was confirmed effective), and any patient-result impact (results held, recalled, corrected).

Errors in reported patient results trigger: prompt notification of the ordering provider, prompt issuance of a corrected report, and retention of both the original and the corrected report per 42 CFR 493.1282(a)(3).
Corrective action records are reviewed monthly by the medical director or designee as part of the quality management plan; trends are analyzed and systemic actions taken where indicated.
Corrective action records are retained for at least 2 years per 42 CFR 493.1105.
procedure_steps: Any staff member who identifies a quality issue (QC out, PT failure, complaint, error in reported result, near-miss) opens a Corrective Action record in the lab's quality system or on the paper Corrective Action form.
The record captures the trigger, the date and time, and the immediate containment action (hold patient results, notify the ordering provider, retest, etc.).
The technical consultant or technical supervisor performs root-cause analysis: review of QC, calibration, maintenance, reagent lot, training records, and method.
Corrective action is implemented and documented; the action's effectiveness is verified (subsequent QC in control, subsequent PT successful, subsequent patient results consistent).
If a reported patient result was in error, the ordering provider is notified promptly, a corrected report is issued, both versions are retained, and the patient's chart is updated.
The completed Corrective Action record is filed; the medical director or designee reviews monthly and signs the review.
definitions: Action taken to address a quality finding (QC failure, PT failure, reporting error, complaint, near-miss). Includes immediate containment, root-cause analysis, action to prevent recurrence, and verification of effectiveness.
A patient report issued to replace a previously reported result that was found to be in error. The original report and the corrected report are both retained per 42 CFR 493.1282(a)(3).
The underlying reason a quality finding occurred. Identified through systematic analysis (5-whys, fishbone, fault tree, etc.) rather than surface-level fix.
